headline: Cutting Vines Helps Recovering Forests Grow Faster, Study Finds
meta_description: Research in Borneo shows that removing vining plants helps degraded forests recover faster and absorb more carbon.
summary_short: Cutting back climbing vines dramatically speeds forest recovery in degraded areas, according to new research in Borneo.
body:
As the world grapples with climate change and the loss of biodiversity, scientists are working to understand the best ways to restore forests. A new study published in Current Biology offers a practical insight: removing vining plants called lianas can significantly accelerate forest recovery.

Researchers studied this technique in Borneo, where degraded forests are being reclaimed. By removing the lianas that compete with and strangle recovering trees, they found the forest could grow back faster and stronger. This matters because healthy, dense forests are crucial for absorbing carbon dioxide and providing habitat for wildlife.

The finding adds a straightforward tool to the restoration toolkit: sometimes the fastest path to renewal is simply clearing away the obstacles that slow growth. As forests worldwide face pressure, these practical insights help land managers make choices that benefit both carbon storage and the return of biodiversity.
